thread.title: Russian poll observer Levichev praises EC’s untiring efforts
thread.title_full: Russian poll observer Levichev praises EC’s untiring efforts

author: sulochana ramiah mohan
title: Russian poll observer Levichev praises EC’s untiring efforts
text:
By Sulochana Ramiah Mohan
For the first time, Nikolai Levichev, a member of the Russian Central Election Commission, was invited to observe Sri Lanka’s General Elections.
“I visited your wonderful country for the first time 13 years ago. My first impressions matched my expectations—it is heaven on Earth,” Levichev, who had previously visited Sri Lanka six times, told Ceylon Today.
He commended the tireless efforts of the Election Commission, highlighting the sleepless nights its staff endured, which were also noted by other international observers.
The Parliamentary Elections in Sri Lanka adhered to all international norms of the democratic electoral process, according to Levichev, who arrived in the island on 13 November 2024, as part of the observer mission.
Assigned to the Eastern Province, Levichev had realised a notable gap in voter registration practices. He observed that voter lists in Sri Lanka are updated three times a year, leaving young citizens who turn 18 during the four-month freeze period ineligible to vote. In Trincomalee District alone, 1,790 such citizens were unable to cast their votes despite being constitutionally eligible.
Explaining how he was invited to Sri Lanka, Levichev mentioned that the Chairman of Sri Lanka’s Election Commission had participated in the Russian Presidential Elections in March 2024 as part of the international observer team in Moscow. “We are ready to continue this cooperation on a permanent basis,” Levichev said. He noted that Russia uses the electronic state system GAS “Elections,” which is highly secure and allows real-time public display of vote counts online after polling ends.
Levichev emphasised that democratic elections must comply with principles of universal, equal, and direct suffrage through secret ballots, all of which are enshrined in Sri Lanka’s electoral laws. He added, “In the Eastern Province, particularly in Trincomalee, I observed that these democratic principles were fully respected. Sri Lanka’s cultural and political traditions of preserving electoral sovereignty are deeply commendable.”
Beyond his role as an observer, Levichev attended an event organised by the Association of Graduates of Universities of Socialist Countries. “I was delighted to report to my colleagues in Moscow that Sri Lanka remembers the Soviet Union with gratitude and holds Russia in warm regard,” he said.
Differences between polls in Russia and SL
Discussing differences between elections in Russia and Sri Lanka, Levichev admitted the complexities of comparative analysis. “The differences in electoral procedures are significant, and a detailed analysis would require professional expertise,” he said, adding that he had established strong relationships with Sri Lankan election officials.
When asked about areas for improvement in Sri Lanka’s electoral process, Levichev refrained from giving direct advice but expressed concern about the working conditions of polling staff.
He suggested exploring measures to ease the burden on election officials, who often work for three days with minimal rest, sacrificing their health to fulfil their civic duties.
Levichev expressed confidence in the integrity of the elections, noting, “From the perspective of international standards and Sri Lanka’s electoral sovereignty, all key parameters of democratic elections—universal, equal, and direct suffrage by secret ballot—were observed. The results inspire confidence and reflect the will of the people.”
During his observations, Levichev noted that most polling stations were staffed predominantly by women. “The electoral process in Sri Lanka has a ‘woman’s face,’” he remarked. He also highlighted the resilience of polling staff, who only learned of their assignments on the morning of Election Day and managed their duties under challenging conditions.
Interview with TASS
Levichev also spoke to TASS (TASS Russian News Agency) after he arrived in Russia. Speaking to TASS, he said, “From the point of view of the procedures within the framework of the electoral sovereignty of Sri Lanka of the International standard, I can say that according to the main parameters there are all the signs of democratic elections – universal equal direct suffrage by secret ballot.”
The Parliamentary Elections took place on Thursday, allowing approximately 17 million Sri Lankan citizens to elect 225 members to Parliament for a five-year term. According to the Election Commission’s data, the National People’s Power (NPP), led by President Anura Kumara Dissanayake and the Janatha Vimukthi Peramuna alliance, won a landslide victory with 61.56 per cent of the vote (6.8 million votes), securing 159 seats.
The main Opposition, the United People’s Force (UPF), led by Sajith Premadasa, received 17.66 per cent of the vote (1.9 million votes), securing 40 seats compared to 54 in the previous Parliament. The Tamil National Party won eight seats, while nine other parties secured between one and five seats each. Polling proceeded peacefully, with no incidents reported. Levichev and other international observers, including representatives from India, the Maldives, and Bangladesh, confirmed that the elections were conducted in compliance with local laws. “Everything proceeded calmly, and I witnessed no irregularities,” Levichev said.
Polling concluded at 4:00 p.m. local time, followed by vote counting. A total of 690 political parties and independent groups contested across 25 districts. The election saw the rise of the People’s Liberation Front, led by Dissanayake, which focused on poverty alleviation as Sri Lanka recovers from its financial crisis.
The elections followed a period of political and economic turmoil in Sri Lanka. The country experienced its worst economic crisis in 2021, driven by the collapse of tourism due to the Covid-19 pandemic. Foreign currency shortages led to severe resource constraints, including food, fuel, and medicine shortages, triggering mass protests in 2022. This unrest culminated in the resignation of then-President Gotabaya Rajapaksa. Ranil Wickremesinghe served as interim President before Dissanayake was elected in September 2024.
(amiesulo@gmail.com)
